commitments.text: A judge must not frame an innocent person, even if doing so would save five hostages.
A trolley driver of a runaway trolley must divert it from five workmen onto one workman.
A bystander at a switch may divert the trolley from five workmen onto one workman.
A bystander on a bridge must not push a fat man onto the track to stop the trolley, even though this would save five workmen.
A bystander who can divert the trolley either onto one workman or onto five workmen must not divert it onto the five.

theory.text: It is permissible to perform an action that minimizes harm, unless that action involves directly harming someone as a means to achieving a good outcome.

theory.text: It is permissible to perform an action that minimizes harm, unless that action involves directly harming someone as a means to achieving a good outcome.

account.rationale: The theory T is empty, so it entails nothing. Therefore, it contradicts no commitments. However, T is silent on all commitments [IC1], [IC2], [IC3], [IC4], and [IC5]. Since T should be addressing these commitments, the account score is 0.0.

systematicity.rationale: The theory is empty, containing no principles. Therefore, it cannot unify any moral propositions or provide any explanatory reach. An empty theory has minimal systematicity.

faithfulness.rationale: The agent has perfectly preserved all initial commitments. IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4 and IC5 are all maintained without any denials or modifications. Therefore, the faithfulness score is 1.0.

account.rationale: The theory T aligns well with most commitments. It entails IC2 and IC3, as diverting the trolley minimizes harm without directly harming anyone as a means. It also entails IC5, as diverting onto five is not minimizing harm. However, T contradicts IC1 and IC4 because in both cases directly harming someone is a means to a good outcome, which the theory prohibits, even though in both cases harm is minimized.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T presents a single principle [P1] that attempts to unify moral permissibility based on minimizing harm. However, the exception clause regarding direct harm introduces a degree of case-specificity, reducing the overall generality and systematicity. While aiming for broad applicability, the constraint limits its unifying power.

faithfulness.rationale: The agent has perfectly preserved all initial commitments. IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4, and IC5 are all retained without modification or denial. Therefore, the agent exhibits maximal faithfulness to its initial moral intuitions.

trace.proposal_diff: Introduce a principle permitting actions that minimize harm, except where they involve directly harming someone as a means to an end. This addresses the trolley cases (IC2, IC3, IC5) and the framing case (IC1) while respecting the constraint against using people as means (IC4).

commitments.text: A judge must not frame an innocent person, even if doing so would save five hostages, unless the judge has no other option to save the hostages.
A trolley driver of a runaway trolley must divert it from five workmen onto one workman.
A bystander at a switch may divert the trolley from five workmen onto one workman.
A bystander on a bridge must not push a fat man onto the track to stop the trolley, even though this would save five workmen, unless there is no other way to stop the trolley.
A bystander who can divert the trolley either onto one workman or onto five workmen must not divert it onto the five.

theory.text: It is permissible to perform an action that minimizes harm, unless that action involves directly harming someone as a means to achieving a good outcome.

account.rationale: The theory T aligns well with most commitments. It entails IC2 and IC3, as diverting the trolley minimizes harm without directly harming anyone as a means. It also entails IC5, as diverting onto five is not minimizing harm. However, T contradicts IC1 and IC4 because in both cases directly harming someone is a means to a good outcome, which the theory prohibits, even though in both cases harm is minimized.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T presents a single principle [P1] that attempts to unify moral permissibility based on minimizing harm. However, the exception clause regarding direct harm introduces a degree of case-specificity, reducing the overall generality and systematicity. While aiming for broad applicability, the constraint limits its unifying power.

faithfulness.rationale: The agent has perfectly preserved all initial commitments. IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4, and IC5 are all retained without modification or denial. Therefore, the agent exhibits maximal faithfulness to its initial moral intuitions.

account.rationale: The theory T aligns with IC2, IC3, and IC5, as it permits minimizing harm when it doesn't involve directly harming someone as a means. However, T contradicts IC4, which prohibits pushing a fat man even when it's the only way to save five, because pushing the man is directly harming him as a means. T is also silent on IC1, as it does not explicitly address framing an innocent person.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T contains one principle, P1, which attempts to unify permissibility based on minimizing harm while incorporating a constraint against instrumental harm. This provides some generality and explanatory reach, but the exception regarding direct harm introduces some case-specificity, reducing the overall systematicity. It is not a perfect unifying principle, but it's better than a list of cases.

faithfulness.rationale: The agent maintains commitments IC2, IC3, and IC5 without alteration. IC1 and IC4 are reformulated with exceptions ('unless the judge has no other option' and 'unless there is no other way to stop the trolley'). These weaken the original commitments but do not outright deny them, so faithfulness is reduced but not eliminated.

trace.proposal_diff: The theory prohibits directly harming someone as a means to achieving a good outcome. IC1 and IC4 involve directly harming someone as a means. I will reformulate IC1 and IC4 to align with the theory.

commitments.text: A judge must not frame an innocent person, even if doing so would save five hostages.

theory.text: It is permissible to perform an action that minimizes harm, unless that action involves directly causing harm to someone as a means to achieving a good outcome. Allowing harm to occur as a means to minimizing overall harm is permissible.

account.rationale: The theory T aligns well with most commitments. It entails IC2 and IC3, as diverting the trolley minimizes harm without directly harming anyone as a means. It also entails IC5, as diverting onto five is not minimizing harm. However, T contradicts IC1 and IC4 because in both cases directly harming someone is a means to a good outcome, which the theory prohibits, even though in both cases harm is minimized.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T presents a single principle [P1] that attempts to unify moral permissibility based on minimizing harm. However, the exception clause regarding direct harm introduces a degree of case-specificity, reducing the overall generality and systematicity. While aiming for broad applicability, the constraint limits its unifying power.

faithfulness.rationale: The agent has perfectly preserved all initial commitments. IC1, IC2, IC3, IC4, and IC5 are all retained without modification or denial. Therefore, the agent exhibits maximal faithfulness to its initial moral intuitions.

account.rationale: The theory T aligns well with most of the commitments. T entails IC2 and IC3 because diverting the trolley involves allowing harm to one to save five, which is permissible. T entails IC5 because diverting onto one is minimizing harm. T entails IC4 because pushing the man involves directly causing harm as a means. However, T contradicts IC1, because framing an innocent person involves directly causing harm as a means, but the commitment says it is impermissible even to save five hostages. Thus, the score is reduced.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T has a single principle [P1] that attempts to unify permissibility based on minimizing harm, which increases systematicity. However, [P1] includes an exception regarding directly causing harm as a means, which adds complexity and reduces the theory's overall generality. The distinction between directly causing harm and allowing harm is a reasonable attempt at unification, but it introduces some case-specificity.

faithfulness.rationale: The current commitments C are identical to the initial commitments C₀. Therefore, all initial commitments are preserved. There are no denials or abandonments of initial commitments, so the faithfulness score is 1.0.

trace.proposal_diff: Revised P1 to explicitly distinguish between causing harm and allowing harm. This addresses the conflict with IC1 and IC4 by allowing actions that minimize harm even if they involve directly *allowing* harm to occur to someone as a means to achieving a good outcome, but still prohibiting directly *causing* harm as a means.

A judge must frame an innocent person if a supervillain will kill five hostages unless the judge frames someone, and the supervillain will kill the innocent person regardless of whether the judge frames them.
A bystander on a bridge must push a fat man onto the track to stop the trolley if the fat man is already falling and pushing him is the only way to stop the trolley and save five workmen.

However, it contradicts IC4, as pushing the fat man is directly harming him. The theory also contradicts EC2, as pushing the falling man is directly harming him. The theory is silent on IC1 and EC1.

systematicity.rationale: The theory T has one principle, P1, that attempts to unify permissibility based on minimizing harm while incorporating a constraint against instrumental harm. This principle has reasonable generality, but the exception introduces some case-specificity, reducing the overall systematicity compared to a theory with a single, exceptionless principle.

faithfulness.rationale: All initial commitments (IC1-IC5) are preserved in the current commitments. The emerging commitments (EC1, EC2) do not contradict any of the initial commitments. Therefore, the agent maintains perfect faithfulness to their original moral intuitions.

trace.proposal_diff: The verifier noted that the current theory contradicts IC1 and IC4. I will add two emerging commitments that are variants of these cases, but where the direct harm is not a means to achieving the good outcome. This will help us explore the boundaries of the 'means' condition.
